coredata.dc:title: Seeking materials from nature for interrupting eye damage: Ultraviolet to blue light blocking clear cellulose films enabled by curcumin
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Qiu
Qin, Ying
Zhang, Xinjia
Sun, Lijian
Chang, Jiang
coredata.dc:description: The harms caused by ultraviolet (UV) and blue light to eyes are attracting momentous concern due to growing exposure to artificial illumination and modern IT devices. Herein, a simple and eco-friendly adsorption approach was employed to integrate curcumin, a natural bioactive compound, into the cellulose substrate for the development of flexible and biodegradable filters capable of blocking harmful light. The curcumin/cellulose films demonstrate excellent UV-screening competence and photostability, with UV-A and UV-B screening ratios ranging from 92.8 % to 100 % and 89.2 % to 100 %, respectively. The films could block >96 % of blue light in the wavelength range of 400–500 nm. Meanwhile, the films maintain high transmittance (85.2–89.4 %) and low haze (2.0–2.7 %). The films can efficiently block blue light emanated from sunlight, light-emitting diodes, lighting systems, computer and mobile phone screens. Encouragingly, the incorporation of curcumin led to a substantial increase in the water contact angle, elevating it from 41.6 to 81.3°. Furthermore, the films exhibit excellent antimicrobial properties, biodegradability, and tensile strength in excess of 72 MPa. Therefore, these films fabricated entirely from natural resources have the potential to achieve practical applications such as food packaging and spectacle lens, especially suitable for electronic screen protectors.

head.ce:title: Seeking materials from nature for interrupting eye damage: Ultraviolet to blue light blocking clear cellulose films enabled by curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Textiles, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, Heilongjiang Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Light Industry and Textiles, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, Heilongjiang Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The harms caused by ultraviolet (UV) and blue light to eyes are attracting momentous concern due to growing exposure to artificial illumination and modern IT devices. Herein, a simple and eco-friendly adsorption approach was employed to integrate curcumin, a natural bioactive compound, into the cellulose substrate for the development of flexible and biodegradable filters capable of blocking harmful light. The curcumin/cellulose films demonstrate excellent UV-screening competence and photostability, with UV-A and UV-B screening ratios ranging from 92.8 % to 100 % and 89.2 % to 100 %, respectively. The films could block >96 % of blue light in the wavelength range of 400–500 nm. Meanwhile, the films maintain high transmittance (85.2–89.4 %) and low haze (2.0–2.7 %). The films can efficiently block blue light emanated from sunlight, light-emitting diodes, lighting systems, computer and mobile phone screens. Encouragingly, the incorporation of curcumin led to a substantial increase in the water contact angle, elevating it from 41.6 to 81.3°. Furthermore, the films exhibit excellent antimicrobial properties, biodegradability, and tensile strength in excess of 72 MPa. Therefore, these films fabricated entirely from natural resources have the potential to achieve practical applications such as food packaging and spectacle lens, especially suitable for electronic screen protectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Blue light (wavelength of 400–500 nm), as one of the three primary colors of light, is essential in full-color displays, and thus to achieve a colorful picture display, the screen will inevitably emit a certain amount of blue light. Blue light is commonly found in daily life and work, such as all kinds of modern displays, light-emitting diode (LED) lamps, computers, smartphones, and other new man-made light sources emitted by a variety of visible light containing a large number of irregular frequencies of blue light [1,2]. With increasing reliance on displays and artificial lights, humans are increasingly exposed to harmful blue light, and the frequent use of display devices inevitably has an impact on human health and in particular may cause damage to the retina [3,4]. Specifically, prolonged exposure to blue light can lead to retinal pigment epithelial cell dysfunction and even death [5–7]. Nevertheless, it proves challenging to entirely circumvent the hazards posed by UV or blue light while upholding consistent display or lighting quality. Consequently, it becomes imperative to effectively mitigate the risk associated with UV or blue light while ensuring an enjoyable visual experience through the development of materials or techniques capable of obstructing harmful light while maintaining high visible light transmittance and low haze.

It is critical to screen harmful light using adjustable filters with high transmittance and low haze to protect the eyes from these harmful light sources. Furthermore, from an environmental perspective, it is critical to use materials with biocompatibility, biodegradability, and renewability for eco-friendly and sustainable solutions due to microplastic pollution is increasing [8–10]. Polysaccharides are a kind of green polymer, and there is a growing demand for alternatives to chemically synthesized polymers. Different types of polysaccharides (e.g., chitosan, alginate, starch, chitin, and cellulose-based carbohydrate polymers) have been used to manufacture high value-added functional materials. Among them, cellulose film materials are very attractive compared to plastic films derived from petrochemicals due to their abundance, renewability, and biodegradability [11]. Compared with conventional plastics, cellulose film materials can undergo higher processing temperature, which gives them an advantage over some synthetic plastics [11,12]. Consequently, the film derived from cellulose can serve as suitable carrier for incorporating non-toxic and effective harmful light absorbers, allowing the production of protective filters against harmful light. As is known to all, inorganic and organic UV and blue light absorbers have been commonly utilized in the field of harmful light filtering [13–17]. However, some of the synthetic organic UV and blue light absorbers were unstable, generating photonic radicals that caused damage to skin cells [18]. Some materials from nature such as lignin, tea extracts, and henna extracts presented interesting UV-shielding properties [19–21]. Inorganic UV or blue light absorbers such as TiO2, CdS, ZnO, and NiOx nanoparticles have been extensively employed, and excellent light screening performance was actualized [22–25]. However, with the absorption of UV light, the formation of free radicals can attack the polymer structure, leading to subsequent photo-oxidative degradation [26]. Meanwhile, poor dispersion and aggregation of nanoparticles in the films can lead to uneven UV shielding ability, reduced light transmission, and severe impacts on physical strength and biodegradability, thus limiting the wide range of applications of films. There are several works on the employ of cellulose film materials consisting of UV or blue light absorbers for light shielding applications, but these materials have low optical transmittance, high haze, and are unable to fully shield UV and blue light [12,26–28]. For these reasons, the development and enhancement of high-performance UV and blue light-blocking film materials remain necessary to protect human health, particularly in terms of eye protection.

Curcumin is an orange-yellow pigment extracted from the roots of turmeric and other plants in the ginger family with antitumor, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties [29]. Meanwhile, curcumin has attracted much attention because of its excellent photosensitivity. Due to the excellent photosensitivity of curcumin, it has been used in various food systems [30]. Ma et al. [31] applied curcumin as a natural filler to fabricate cellulose-based composite films with excellent photoinduced antibacterial capability. Curcumin is also used as an acid-base indicator [32,33]. Meanwhile, curcumin can effectively absorb UV rays through the interaction of carbon-carbon double bonds, conjugated π-electrons, and carbonyl groups [34,35]. Moreover, the stability of curcumin is not significantly affected by UV radiation [36]. To the best of our knowledge, the production of cellulose-based films incorporating curcumin as harmful light filters to mitigate eye damage induced by artificial light sources and electronic devices has not been systematically reported and studied.

Herein, curcumin and cellulose, derived exclusively from renewable and biodegradable sources, are integrated into a novel type of functional film for mitigating eye damage induced by modern IT devices and artificial light sources, which has broad application prospects. The hypothesis posits that curcumin has the potential to confer upon cellulose films the ability to filter harmful light, while preserving high visible light transmittance and low haze of films. By altering the content of curcumin in the films, we can obtain films with high transmittance (89.4–85.2 %), low haze (2.0–2.7 %), and varying degrees of UV to blue light shielding. In practical application, the composite films can proficiently screen blue light emanated from light-emitting diodes, lighting systems, sunlight, computer screens, and mobile phone screens. The incorporation of curcumin led to a substantial rise in the water contact angle, elevating it from 41.6 to 81.3°. Additionally, it is noteworthy that the composite films exhibit a robust antibacterial impact on E. coli and S. aureus, with an antibacterial ratio surpassing 90 % for both bacteria. Moreover, these films retain high tensile strength (>72 MPa). Our findings provide a new option to replace the existing non-degradable anti-blue light films to slow down retinal damage. This work presents a facile and environmentally friendly strategy for the fabrication of highly transparent, antibacterial, low haze, high strength, and hazardous light screening cellulose-based composite films.

Quantitative filter paper was acquired from Hangzhou Special Paper Industry Co., Ltd. (China). Curcumin (98 % purity) and sodium chloroacetate (MCA, 98 % purity) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China. Lithium hydroxide hydrate (LiOH·H2O, 99 % purity) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Cellulase (0.7 U/mg) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Urea (99 % purity), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97 % purity), and anhydrous ethanol were acquired from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China).

A grinder model A11 (IKA, Germany) was used to grind small pieces of filter paper into powder for later use. Filter paper (40 g) was introduced into 1000 mL 1 M solution of MCA prepared in 15 % (w/v) NaOH and stirred for 2 h at 50 °C. Subsequently, the carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were isolated through filtration. To achieve neutral condition, the fibers were subjected to three washes with tap water, effectively eliminating any remaining unreacted residues. The carboxymethylated fibers are then washed with distilled water and dried at 65 °C. The content of carboxyl groups in virgin cellulose and carboxymethyl cellulose fibers was determined [37,38].

Briefly, 16 g LiOH·H2O and 30 g urea were added to 154 g of distilled water [39,40]. Then, 8 g of carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were introduced to the above solution and stirred for 2 min. After carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were evenly dispersed in the solution, the mixture was frozen in the refrigerator at −20 °C for 6 h. Subsequently, the mixture was thawed at 25 °C to form a 4 wt% carboxymethylated cellulose solution. The resulting transparent carboxymethylated cellulose solution was placed in an ultrasonic cleaner and sonicated for 20 min under ice-water bath conditions to remove bubbles. The carboxymethylated cellulose solution was poured into a glass tank (60 × 26 × 1.5 cm) and left undisturbed for a period of time, then anhydrous ethanol (250 mL) was added to the glass tank to regenerate and solidify the carboxymethylated cellulose solution [41,42]. Subsequently, the regenerated carboxymethylated cellulose was subjected to multiple washes with distilled water to yield hydrogel film (1.2 mm thickness).

The carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogels were soaked in ethanol multiple times to facilitate water removal through exchange. Different amounts of curcumin (0.01 g, 0.02 g, and 0.04 g) were added to 100 mL of ethanol to form curcumin-ethanol solutions. The gels (8 × 8 cm) were soaked in curcumin-ethanol solutions at 25 °C for 2 h. Anhydrous ethanol was used to rinse the surface of the gels. The curcumin/carboxymethylated cellulose gels were affixed to glass plates to prevent shrinkage and air-dried at 25 °C, resulting in curcumin/carboxymethylated cellulose films with a thickness of approximately 0.12 mm. These composite films were assigned the codes Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3. Additionally, a curcumin-free carboxymethylated cellulose film (CCF, 0.12 mm) was also produced. The content of curcumin in the films was calculated from a calibration curve using a TU-1950 spectrophotometer [43]. After calculation, the content of curcumin is 6.47 mg/g, 10.63 mg/g, and 13.38 mg/g in Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV and blue light barrier properties of films and evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis transmittance spectra of Cur-CCFs were investigated using UV–vis spectrophotometry. The UV–vis transmittance spectra were utilized to evaluate the UV and blue light blocking capability of Cur-CCFs. The UV and blue light screening effectiveness of Cur-CCFs was evaluated based on the subsequent equations.

UV-A screening (320–400 nm) ratio was calculated using Eq. (1).

In practical scenarios where UV and blue light screening is required [from WLEDs in mobile phone screens, computer screens, and lighting systems, as well as the blue light emitted by the sunshine], measurements were conducted using CCF and Cur-CCFs placed between OHSP350 spectrometer and light sources.

The UV test card was employed to validate the UV-screening ability of CCF and Cur-CCFs. A UV lamp (WFH-204B, 365 nm) was employed to excite the UV test card both with and without film coverage, and corresponding photographs were captured.

The water contact angle (WCA) test is employed to estimate the surface wettability of prepared CCF and Cur-CCFs. The WCA measurement was carried out using the JY-82B video contact angle tester (Chengdu Lingdu Instrument Co., Ltd., China) at room temperature. An automatic micro-syringe was employed to deposit 5 μL of distilled water on CCF and Cur-CCFs surface, and then the photographs were collected. The static WCA was acquired by droplet shape analysis.

The enzymatic degradation approach was employed to assess the biodegradability of CCF and Cur-CCFs. The experiment was conducted using a cellulase enzyme (0.7 U/mg, Sigma-Aldrich) in an acetate buffer (pH = 5) at 37 °C. 80 mg of CCF and Cur-CCFs were added in 20 mL of 0.1 mg/mL cellulase enzyme-acetate buffer solution for a certain interval. After the incubation time was reached, the specimens were washed with deionized water and vacuum-dried at 35 °C. The enzymatic degradation result was expressed as the weight loss percentage,

where m 0 and m d are the weights of CCF and Cur-CCFs before and after enzymatic degradation measurements, respectively.

Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Waltham, USA) was applied to evaluate the chemical structures of specimens using 32 repetitious scans and 4 cm−1 resolution. The crystalline structures of samples were determined using a D/max2200VPC X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) system. The diffraction angle range examined was from 5 to 50°. The samples were observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S4800, Hitachi Corporation, Japan) to determine surface and cross-sectional morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of samples was recorded on the ESCALAB250Xi instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a STA449 F3 thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH, Germany). The tensile testing of samples was conducted on a UTM2203 universal testing machine (Shenzhen SUNS Technology Stock Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, China) at a speed of 2 mm min−1. The samples with a size of 60 mm × 6 mm were used in tensile tests. A gauge length of 25 mm was maintained. Before testing, the films are placed in sealed containers containing saturated salt solution to obtain microclimates with relative humidity (RH) between 50 % and 90 %. The samples were then equilibrated in the container at 30 °C for 10 days. The UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was applied to test transmittance and haze. The OHSP350 spectrometer (Hangzhou HOPOO Light Color Technology Co., Ltd., China) was utilized for the acquisition of LED parameters.

All experiments were conducted in triplicate, with results expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). The experimental data were processed and analyzed using Origin V8.0 software, while statistical analysis was conducted through one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) utilizing GraphPad V8.0.2 and SPSS 26.0, with the significance level established at P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication process and characterization of Cur-CCFs
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of curcumin/cellulose films (Cur-CCFs) with hazardous light screening capability utilizing a facile and environmentally friendly adsorption process is presented in Fig. 1a. Since 2005, LiOH/urea aqueous solution has been shown to dissolve cellulose effectively and has been successfully used in the production of regenerated cellulose [39]. Indeed, achieving high transparency in cellulose films is relatively straightforward, but attaining low haze is challenging. Our previous research revealed that treating cellulose fibers with carboxymethylation during the preparation of cellulose film leads to notable alterations in its optical properties, including a substantial enhancement in transparency and a significant reduction in haze [42]. Consequently, the carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were synthesized to improve the visible transmittance and reduce the haze of the films. The methylene blue method was employed to measure the content of carboxyl groups. Carboxymethylation resulted in a significant increase in the carboxyl group content, from 5.25 ± 0.12 mmol/kg in virgin cellulose fibers to 165.34 ± 1.65 mmol/kg in carboxymethylated cellulose fibers. The carboxymethylated cellulose fibers were dissolved to form a highly transparent carboxymethylated cellulose solution, and it was observed that the carboxymethylated cellulose solution had higher transmittance and lower haze than the solution prepared from unmodified cellulose fibers (Fig. S1). Following regeneration, the cellulose solution was converted into cellulose hydrogel, which served as the substrate for the adsorption of curcumin molecules, resulting in the production of cellulose-based functional films. Fig. 1c illustrates that the carboxymethylated cellulose hydrogel exhibits high transmittance and low haze. Despite the yellow coloration observed in the composite films upon curcumin incorporation, they retained their high transmittance and low haze characteristics (Fig. 1d).

FTIR spectra of three samples were acquired and depicted in Fig. 2a. The pristine fibers demonstrate two prominent absorption peaks at 2899 and 3336 cm−1, corresponding to asymmetric −CH stretching and −OH stretching, respectively. The stretching vibration of the CO bond is responsible for the absorption peak at 1644 cm−1. The absorption peak observed at 1021 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the CO bond [44]. For carboxymethylated cellulose fibers, a new characteristic peak at 1590 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO (COOH) stretch, manifesting the successful grafting of carboxyl groups onto the cellulose skeleton [42]. CCF also showed a CO absorption peak at 1590 cm−1. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectrum of curcumin demonstrates many characteristic absorption peaks, which are ascribed to the existence of various functional groups in the molecular structure of curcumin. The peak was observed at 3505 cm−1 ascribed to the phenol–OH group stretching. The peaks at 1628 cm−1, 1510 cm−1, and 1429 cm−1 correspond to conjugated CO stretching, CC stretching, and CH bending vibration, respectively. The appearance of the peak at 1283 cm−1 corresponds to the tensile vibration of –C–O. The characteristic absorption peaks that arise at 963, 814, and 714 cm−1 were associated with the CH bending vibration of the alkene groups [45,46]. Compared to CCF, the peak positions in the Cur-CCFs spectra exhibited no significant alteration, thereby manifesting that the inclusion of curcumin did not change cellulose structure. In the Cur-CCFs, the stretching peak of –OH at 3352 cm−1 was observed to exhibit increased width and reduced intensity relative to that in CCF [47]. These characteristics indicate an enhancement in hydrogen bonding of the hydroxyl groups, which seems to facilitate the interaction between curcumin and cellulose [47]. A possible scheme of hydrogen bond interaction between cellulose and curcumin is displayed in Fig. 2c. The XPS spectra of samples are displayed in Fig. S2. Fig. S2a illustrates that CCF and Cur-CCFs exclusively consist of carbon and oxygen constituents, wherein the peaks corresponding to C1s and O1s are positioned at 284.8 eV and 532.1 eV. The high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s in Cur-CCFs exhibit negligible alterations compared to CCF, indicating solely the presence of physical interactions between cellulose and curcumin (Fig. S2b and c).

XRD was employed to analyze the crystalline structure of CCF, curcumin, and Cur-CCFs. As shown in Fig. 2d, the XRD pattern of the CCF featured three distinctive diffraction peaks at 12.4°, 20.2°, and 22.2°, which can be attributed to the diffraction of (1-10), (110), and (020) faces of cellulose II type. Curcumin is highly crystalline, as has been reported in previous literature [48,49]. Fig. 2d reveals that pure curcumin powder exhibited obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.83°, 8.78°, 12.1°, 15.7°, 17.13°, and 21.01°, which align with the XRD pattern of curcumin as reported in previous study [48]. The XRD pattern of Cur-CCFs is similar to that of CCF. It may also be that curcumin is less abundant in the Cur-CCFs.

The TGA and DTG curves of CCF and Cur-CCFs are shown in Fig. 2e and f. Three stages of weight loss were identified within the temperature range of 40 to 600 °C. At around 40–160 °C, a minor decrease in mass was noted for both CCF and Cur-CCFs, attributed to the loss of moisture from these materials. The primary weight loss stage occurs between 253 and 355 °C, constituting 48 % of the overall weight loss, primarily attributed to the degradation of cellulose. The third stage commences at 352 °C and extends till 600 °C, representing the decomposition of the residual substances. The temperature at which curcumin exhibits its highest degradation rate is approximately 395 °C, surpassing that of regenerated CCF (355 °C). The char residue of curcumin is much higher than that of CCF, which should be due to the phenyl rings in curcumin. All Cur-CCFs exhibited thermal stability up to 352 °C, which is near that of CCF.

The cross-sectional and surface SEM images of CCF and Cur-CCFs are shown in Figs. 2g–j and S3. In our previous works, the prepared CCF had a highly uniform and dense surface structure [50]. A similar surface structure was also discovered in this work, as displayed in Fig. S3. The SEM images demonstrated that all Cur-CCFs had no visible cracks and holes on their surface, but relatively smooth and homogeneous structures. It may be due to the low content of curcumin and the good compatibility between curcumin and cellulose, no small particles of curcumin crystals were observed on the surface of Cur-CCFs after the evaporation of solvent during the film-making process. Fig. 2g–j reveals that the cross-sectional morphology of Cur-CCFs exhibits a relatively dense, uniform, and continuous structure. The introduction of curcumin had no impact on the cross-sectional morphology of Cur-CCFs, indicating a consistent and homogeneous dispersion of curcumin within the films. Despite the high curcumin content in Cur-CCF3, there were no visible curcumin aggregation or dispersed particles. The previous literature reports have shown that the host materials played critical roles in inhibiting the apparent crystallization or agglomeration during the manufacturing process of hybrid films [30,51,52].

The optical performance of CCF and Cur-CCFs is displayed in Fig. 3 . CCF and Cur-CCFs are transparent, where CCF is colorless and Cur-CCFs exhibited a yellow color due to the introduction of curcumin (Fig. 1d). The UV–vis light transmittance curves of films were measured (Fig. 3a). CCF has high transmittance in UV-A, UV-B, and visible light regions, demonstrating that CCF without curcumin is not effective in shielding harmful light. As displayed in Fig. 3a, with the increase of curcumin content in the cellulose matrix, the harmful light screening potential of Cur-CCFs also gradually increased. Table S1 displays the transmittance (at 600 nm) of films in comparison with UV and blue light blocking capabilities. The screening ratios of Cur-CCFs for UV and blue light are shown in Fig. 3b. The Cur-CCF1 blocks ∼97.4 % of UV-A, ∼99.2 % of UV-B, and ∼96.1 % of blue light (400–500 nm). The Cur-CCF2 blocks ∼99.1 % UV-A, ∼99.7 % of UV-B, and ∼97.8 % of blue light. Cur-CCF3 can almost completely block UV and blue light regions along with ∼85.3 % visible light transmittance. The light shielding ratios of CCFs and Cur-CCFs from the emission spectral intensities of CCF and Cur-CCFs when blocking 395 nm and 450 nm light-emitting diode chips were calculated. CCF has almost no shielding effect on 395 nm and 460 nm light. The UV light screening ratios of glass, CCF, and Cur-CCFs are 5.1 % (glass), 22.5 % (CCF), 94.3 % (Cur-CCF1), 98.6 % (Cur-CCF2), and 100 % (Cur-CCF3), respectively, as shown in Fig. 3c. The blue light screening ratios of the glass, CCF, and Cur-CCFs are 2.3 % (glass), 8.2 % (CCF), 100 % (Cur-CCF1), 100 % (Cur-CCF2), and 100 % (Cur-CCF3), respectively, as shown in Fig. 3d. When the curcumin content in solution is 0.01 wt%, the light shielding ratios of 395 nm and 450 nm are 94.3 % and 100 %, respectively. As the further increase of curcumin content, both 395 nm and 450 nm light are completely shielded. Table S2 provides a comparative analysis of transmittance and UV-blocking performance of Cur-CCF3 in relation to film materials studied in previous literature [12,21,53–58]. Table S3 presents a comparative evaluation of blue light-blocking capability and transmittance properties of Cur-CCF3 with harmful light shielding material mentioned in other works [7,17,42,57,59–61]. These results suggest that Cur-CCF3 has a better light-blocking capability compared to those thin films. The superior UV-screening performance of Cur-CCFs was additionally confirmed through the utilization of the UV test card (Fig. 3e). When the UV test card was covered by CCF, the color of the card remained unaltered, suggesting the ineffectiveness of CCF in UV protection. When the UV test cards were covered by Cur-CCFs, the color of the covered area on the cards remained unchanged, suggesting that Cur-CCFs have excellent UV-blocking ability. The long-term application of Cur-CCFs necessitates the imperative stability of their UV-screening performance. Cur-CCF3 consistently exhibited exceptional hazardous light screening performance for a duration of 12 h under continuous UV light irradiation (85 W, 365 nm), as depicted in Fig. S4. Curcumin is a rare diketone natural pigment with excellent UV shielding properties due to the presence of both benzene ring and ketocarbonyl structures in its molecule [34,35]. Curcumin can effectively absorb a wide range of UV light under the combined action of conjugated π electrons, carbonyl groups, and adjacent carbon‑carbon double bonds. Therefore, UV irradiation has no significant effect on the stability of curcumin [36].

Photographs of blue laser diode (LD < 200 mW, 405 nm), glass-blocked LD, CCF-blocked LD, and Cur-CCFs blocked LD were acquired by implementing the test procedure illustrated in Fig. 4a. Fig. 4b demonstrates the photos of CCF, glass, and Cur-CCFs subjected to irradiation by an LD. Upon utilizing the LD to stimulate the glass and CCF, the intensity of blue light resembles that of LD, indicating the ineffectiveness of CCF and glass in shielding blue light. Nevertheless, due to the efficient absorption of the majority of blue light, no light can traverse through the Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3, as evident to the naked eye.

To further investigate the anti-blue light competence of films, CCF and Cur-CCFs are utilized to block a blue InGaN LED chip (blue LED, 460 nm). Fig. 5a shows photographs of blue LED sheltered by CCF and Cur-CCFs. It is evident from Fig. 5a that the application of CCF on the blue LED results in the observation of intense blue light. Compared with CCF, when Cur-CCFs are used to shield LED, almost no blue light is observed from LED, that is, the blue light intensity is significantly reduced. These electronic gadgets emit small amounts of UV rays and harmful blue light. As everyone knows, blue light hurts the eyes and can cause many eye diseases. The utilization of Cur-CCFs as a desktop computer screen protector, as depicted in Fig. 5b, can effectively mitigate eye damage induced by electronic devices. The presence of Cur-CCF1 covers on the desktop computer screen, as shown in Fig. 5c, does not compromise the legibility of the displayed images. Typically, electronic full-color displays are comprised of red (R), blue (B), and green (G) LEDs [62]. The expectation is that the Cur-CCFs can efficiently obstruct harmful light while allowing the transmission of other wavelengths of visible light. Consequently, the selective blue light screening performance of Cur-CCFs was assessed using three LEDs. Fig. 5d illustrates that the Cur-CCFs exhibit opposite filtering performances for B-, G-, and R-LED. Notably, the emission of the blue light from the B-LED is blocked while the emission of red and green light from the R- and G-LED remained largely unaltered when shielded by Cur-CCF1, demonstrating that Cur-CCF1 is highly selective in blocking blue light.

As a demonstration of practical application, we studied the ability of Cur-CCFs to screen the blue light emanated from sunshine, two types of computers, mobile phone (Fig. 6), and lighting lamp (Fig. S5). The blue light screening ratio of pure CCF for several electronic products is very low, indicating that CCF cannot effectively screen blue light emanated from artificial illumination and modern IT devices. The effect of Cur-CCFs in screening blue light emanated from sunlight, two types of computer screens, mobile phone screen, and lighting lamp gradually improved with an increase in the curcumin content within the cellulose film, that is, the shielding ratio of blue light gradually increased (Fig. 6a–d). The above results are in line with the changes in the intensity of red, blue, and green light in the intensity spectra (Fig. 6e). A blue light test card has also been utilized to test the anti-blue light ability of Cur-CCFs. Video 1 shows that Cur-CCFs significantly block blue light. Video 2 demonstrates the process of using a functional film to block blue light from a smartphone, and a significant reduction in blue light intensity can be directly observed.

As a demonstration of practical application, we studied the ability of Cur-CCFs to screen the blue light emanated from sunshine, two types of computers, mobile phone (Fig. 6), and lighting lamp (Fig. S5). The blue light screening ratio of pure CCF for several electronic products is very low, indicating that CCF cannot effectively screen blue light emanated from artificial illumination and modern IT devices. The effect of Cur-CCFs in screening blue light emanated from sunlight, two types of computer screens, mobile phone screen, and lighting lamp gradually improved with an increase in the curcumin content within the cellulose film, that is, the shielding ratio of blue light gradually increased (Fig. 6a–d). The above results are in line with the changes in the intensity of red, blue, and green light in the intensity spectra (Fig. 6e). A blue light test card has also been utilized to test the anti-blue light ability of Cur-CCFs. Video 1 shows that Cur-CCFs significantly block blue light. Video 2 demonstrates the process of using a functional film to block blue light from a smartphone, and a significant reduction in blue light intensity can be directly observed.

We next analyzed the haze of CCF and Cur-CCFs. In general, lower levels of haze are desirable as screen protectors of electronic products with reduced haze offer improved visual clarity without compromising screen display. Objects viewed through Cur-CCFs were also clearly visible, manifesting that Cur-CCFs possess low haze. The inclusion of curcumin in the films does not have a substantial impact on their optical transmittance and haze (Fig. 7a–d). The haze values of the composite films progressively increase with an increase in curcumin content within the cellulose film, as depicted in Fig. 7e. Compared to other cellulose-based materials, CCF and Cur-CCFs have lower haze, similar to glass (Fig. 7f) [63–71]. Cur-CCFs demonstrate lower haze compared to conventional petroleum-based materials, suggesting their potential as substitutes (Fig. 7g) [42,72]. These results all confirm that Cur-CCFs can serve as harmful light screening films for eye protection. Importantly, due to Cur-CCFs have good blue light absorption capacity, especially low haze, they have many great potential applications where harmful blue light needs to be blocked such as the lens of spectacles.

The utilization of materials in aqueous systems is a common practice. Consequently, certain situations require the materials to possess liquid resistance. The wettability of films was estimated using the WCA test. As depicted in Fig. 8 , the WCA of CCF was measured to be 41.6 ± 0.5, indicating its hydrophobic nature. The WCA of Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3 were found to be 70.2 ± 0.5°, 75.6 ± 0.5°, and 81.3 ± 0.5°, respectively, indicating an enhanced hydrophobicity of Cur-CCFs upon the inclusion of curcumin. The wettability of films can be significantly influenced by the hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity of the filler [73]. The enhanced hydrophobicity of Cur-CCFs, in comparison to CCF, can be primarily attributed to the hydrophobic nature of curcumin [74]. Liu et al. [46] also obtained similar results, the introduction of curcumin can substantially increase the WCA of the cationic starch film. Hasan et al. [75] prepared hydrophobic CNC films via oxa-Michael addition reaction between cellulose and n-benzylmaleimide. After modification, the contact angle of the CNC film was increased from 25.9 ± 4.1° to 57.2 ± 5.5°. Rbihiet et al. [76] fabricated composite films based on TiO2 and cellulose triacetate, and the contact angle and surface free energy of the composite film were tested. Although the large surface free energy of the cellulose/TiO2 composite film, the contact angle is small. Compared to the above, Cur-CCFs exhibit higher contact angles.

The antibacterial activity of Cur-CCFs against S. aureus and E. coli was assessed using the plate counting method. As shown in Fig. 9a and b, the inhibition ratios of Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3 against E. coli were 92.5 %, 94.4 %, and 97.8 %, respectively. The inhibition ratios of Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3 against S. aureus were 93.6 %, 95.8 %, and 98.9 %, respectively. Fig. 9d shows photographs of colonies of both bacteria after exposure of the control group and Cur-CCF1. In contrast, the inhibition ratios of CCF against two kinds of bacteria are not >2 %, demonstrating that the CCF did not inhibit the growth of two species of bacteria. The exposure of Cur-CCF1 led to a substantial reduction in the colony count of two species of bacteria in comparison to the control group, indicating the effective eradication of the majority of E. coli and S. aureus in the suspension owing to the release of curcumin.

A critical criterion for the substitution of synthetic plastics with cellulose films is the requirement of adequate mechanical strength in cellulose films. The tensile properties of the prepared films were tested and are summarized in Fig. 10a–d. The CCF has an average maximum tensile strength of 73.1 MPa, Young's modulus of 2.83 GPa, breaking elongation of 5.8 %, and tensile toughness of 3.7 MJ/m3. The mechanical properties of Cur-CCFs are similar to those of CCF. This result indicated that the incorporation of curcumin did not significantly affect the mechanical properties of Cur-CCFs. The photographs displayed in Fig. 10e illustrate the exceptional flexibility of Cur-CCF3, enabling it to be easily manipulated through bending, twisting, folding, and knotting, thereby enhancing its practical utility. The tensile strength of all films surpassed 72 MPa, a significantly higher value compared to other polymeric matrices (e.g., HDPE, PS, PCL, PHAs, PLAs, PBS, PVC, ABS, and PBAT) employed for the production of optical materials (Fig. S6) [77–84]. Additionally, Cur-CCFs exhibit superior tensile strength compared to some harmful light protective films (e.g., CDs@cel, ANFs/NFC-9 %, HEC/ANF1.0, CNSP-X5, CA/Cu-MOF7.0, CMC/Pal-D1–9%, C-3PMIA, CWNF-4, and NCD@cel3) (Fig. S7) [14,85–91]. Moreover, the tensile strength change of cellulose films under varying humidity conditions was investigated. The tensile strength of pure CCF and Cur-CCF3 under diverse relative humidity levels is depicted in Fig. S8. The results demonstrated a consistent decline in the tensile strength of both CCF and Cur-CCF3 as the relative humidity increased. At elevated humidity levels, Cur-CCF3 exhibits a higher tensile strength compared to CCF.

The enzymatic degradation of CCF and Cur-CCFs was carried out at 37 °C using cellulase to assess the biodegradability of CCF and Cur-CCFs. The biodegradability of CCF and Cur-CCFs is quantified as the percentage of weight loss, as depicted in Fig. 11 . The percentage of mass loss for all samples progressively increases with longer cellulase treatment time. After 12 h of cellulase incubation, the weight losses of CCF, Cur-CCF1, Cur-CCF2, and Cur-CCF3 were 51.5 %, 50.2 %, 49.1 %, and 47.9 %, respectively. Furthermore, the weight losses of CCF and Cur-CCFs exceeded 83 % after 36 h of cellulase incubation. The presence of curcumin has minimal impact on the degradation of Cur-CCFs. These results manifest that CCF and Cur-CCFs have exceptional biodegradability.

We employ curcumin, a natural compound, to confer cellulose films with an exceptional light-screening capability. Upon the adsorption of curcumin, the Cur-CCFs demonstrate favorable characteristics such as high visible light transmittance, low haze, antibacterial and harmful light-screening properties. This UV and blue light screening film could be scaled-up through a facile route. Fig. 12 illustrates a proposed roll-to-roll system, serving as a potential engineering production line of multifunctional films. This system involves the preparation of carboxymethylated cellulose fibers, liquefied cellulose, regenerated cellulose, the adsorption of curcumin, and subsequent washing and drying processes. Therefore, this method is promising for large-area preparation of biodegradable harmful light filter (e.g., Inserted graph). Fig. S9 shows an example of preparative scale-up, where the cellulose gel film was placed in a 50cm × 25 cm × 2 cm glass channel filled with curcumin-ethanol solution and soaked for ∼1 h. After washing and drying, the fabricated cellulose film exhibited well-preserved physical properties. The potential application of Cur-CCFs as harmful light filter is demonstrated as a consequence of screen protective film for desktop computer (Fig. 5c). In conclusion, this highly scalable, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly production process bestows the films with significant practical potential. Despite the promising industrial application of this composite film, the precise regulation of curcumin concentration in industrial production may be a limitation compared to laboratory production, and we will focus on this issue in subsequent work.

In summary, curcumin, a renewable biomass-derived UV and blue light absorber, is embedded in the cellulose matrix to create a flexible film that protects our eyes from harmful light. We have shown a highly transparent and low haze cellulose film with antimicrobial, biodegradability, good mechanical strength, and tunable harmful light screening properties through a simple, green, and scalable strategy. The light-shielding films prepared by the combination of curcumin and cellulose significantly reduce the accumulation of microplastics that are increasingly harmful to the environment, animals, and humans. The curcumin/cellulose films exhibited considerable UV-screening ability and light stability, with UV-A and UV-B screening ratios ranging from 92.8 % to 100 % and 89.2 % to 100 %, respectively. The composite films could block >96 % of blue light in the 400–500 nm wavelength range At the same time, the films maintain high visible light transmittance (85.2–89.4 %) and low haze (2.0–2.7 %). The light-screening effect of these films with UV and blue LEDs, computer screens, mobile phone screens, and lighting systems was also verified. These results demonstrate that the films can effectively reduce photodamage while ensuring a satisfactory visual experience. Furthermore, these films exhibit excellent antimicrobial properties, biodegradability, and tensile strength above 72 MPa. Quite encouragingly, the introduction of curcumin greatly increases the WCA from 41.6 to 81.3°. Thus, the curcumin-tailored high-performance UV to blue light screening clear cellulose films have a wide range of applications in areas such as electronic screen protectors and lens of spectacles.
